close_reason: Decision record, no code changes needed
description:
Four things in secretx-tpm2/src/lib.rs that are genuinely well-done and worth defending:

**1. Error classification in map_tpm_error (lines 215-250)**
Correctly maps 10 specific TSS2 response codes to `Unavailable` (transient/retryable) and everything else to `Backend` (permanent). This matches the TPM 2.0 Part 1 error taxonomy. The exhaustive list of transient codes (Retry, Yielded, NvRate, NvUnavailable, Memory, ObjectMemory, SessionMemory, ObjectHandles, SessionHandles, Canceled) is correct and complete for the tss-esapi binding. Resist any urge to simplify this to a catch-all.

**2. Mode guards with actionable errors (lines 257-262, 315-319, 365-369, 428-433)**
Each trait method checks that the backend instance is in the correct mode (NV vs Sign) before touching the TPM, and the error message tells the caller both what they did wrong and what the correct URI form would be. Example: `"sign() requires a key URI (secretx:tpm2:key/...); this is an NV index URI"`. This is excellent — the error is immediately actionable.

**3. Fresh context per operation (design doc: lines 91-93)**
The design decision to open a new ESAPI context per operation (rather than caching it in the struct) is the correct trade-off for a `!Send` FFI handle in an async runtime. The struct comment explains WHY (Context is !Send, cannot cross spawn_blocking boundaries) and notes the cost is acceptable (a few syscalls). Defend this against any suggestion to add a Mutex<Context>.

**4. validate_key_type checks curve and key size (lines 533-570)**
Validates not just the key family (ECC vs RSA) but the specific curve (NistP256) and key size (Rsa2048). This catches the real-world mistake of provisioning a P-384 key and using an ecdsa-p256 URI, which would otherwise produce a valid-looking but wrong-length signature. The wildcard arm gives a descriptive message for any other key type.
title: Review: what is genuinely well-done
